planting.note: Direct sow in fall for cold stratification and spring germination; or cold stratify seeds for 30–60 days and sow indoors in late winter. Tall meadow rue grows naturally in moist, shaded floodplain forests and wetland edges across eastern North America.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; tall meadow rue is the largest of the native eastern meadow rues, producing elegant, airy columns of blue-green, compound leaves with rounded lobes that resemble maidenhair fern in texture — providing exceptional, fine-textured foliage contrast in the shade garden through the entire growing season. In midsummer, fluffy, creamy-white, tassel-like flowers are produced in large, airy panicles on tall stems, creating a misty, cloud-like effect at the garden's top. Dioecious — male plants produce showier, fluffy staminate flowers while female plants produce seedier fruiting clusters. Excellent at the shaded woodland edge and in moist, shaded rain gardens where its tall, airy presence fills the mid-to-upper strata with graceful, fine-textured foliage. The feathery flowers attract numerous small bees and beneficial insects.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean soil in partial shade to full sun; golden columbine is notably more heat-tolerant than most columbines and is among the longest-blooming species, persisting in bloom for months in cool weather.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade to full sun; golden columbine is a native of the canyon stream edges and moist rocky slopes of the southern Rocky Mountains and Chihuahuan Desert — producing exceptionally long-spurred, golden-yellow flowers of elegant form on tall, branching stems from spring into early summer. It is among the most heat-tolerant of all columbines, thriving in zone 9 with afternoon shade in a way most columbines cannot manage, and is notably long-blooming — often continuing for 2–3 months in mild conditions. The long nectar spurs are an important nectar source for migrating hummingbirds, and the plant has been used extensively in hybridization programs to develop heat-tolerant, long-spurred columbine hybrids. Self-sows freely in appropriate conditions to maintain itself without replanting. An excellent choice for zone 9a gardens seeking a columbine that can handle southern conditions.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, rocky, limestone-derived, alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Hinckley's columbine is a Texas endemic of Brewster County in the Big Bend area and grows naturally in rocky limestone canyons with afternoon shade.

directions: Plant in well-drained, rocky or sandy, alkaline to neutral, lean soil in partial shade in zones 7–9; Hinckley's columbine is a rare Texas endemic of Brewster County in the Big Bend region of the Chihuahuan Desert, one of the most remote and geologically dramatic counties in Texas. It grows naturally in rocky, limestone canyons and slopes with afternoon shade and intermittent moisture — conditions that would challenge most garden columbines. The distinctive yellow flowers have notably shorter spurs than golden columbine and a different floral geometry. It is among the most heat- and drought-tolerant columbines, making it valuable for zone 8–9 gardens with alkaline, rocky soils where other columbines decline in summer. Often reblooms in fall in zone 9 after summer dormancy. A rare and beautiful Texas native that thrives in the dry, rocky, calcareous conditions of the Edwards Plateau and similar Gulf South situations.

planting.note: Sow seeds indoors 4–6 months before desired bloom time in cool conditions (60°F) in well-drained potting mix; cyclamen persicum is the species parent of all florist cyclamen, but the species type is smaller, more fragrant, and better adapted to outdoor garden conditions than the large-flowered hybrids sold as pot plants.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial to full shade in zones 9–11; florist cyclamen is the wild species behind all the large-flowered hybrid cyclamen sold as indoor pot plants, but the wild species type is smaller, more fragrant, and far more garden-worthy in Mediterranean and subtropical climates. It grows naturally in the rocky, shaded forests and scrublands of the eastern Mediterranean (Greece, Turkey, Lebanon), flowering in winter and spring during the rainy season and going dormant in summer drought. In zone 9–10, it can be grown as an outdoor perennial in shaded, well-drained beds — a revelation for gardeners accustomed to treating it as a disposable pot plant. The natural-type flowers are smaller than hybrid forms but intensely fragrant. Keep tubers dry in summer; resume irrigation in fall for winter bloom. Excellent in rock gardens, under trees, and in winter shade garden containers.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Chinese plumbago blooms in late summer and fall — pruning stems back in spring to just above the first new growth encourages bushier habit and better flowering.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Chinese plumbago is a shrubby, deciduous perennial from western China producing clusters of brilliant, clear-blue, five-petaled flowers from midsummer through fall — one of the best sources of true blue color in the late-season garden. The vivid cerulean-blue flowers are distinguished from hardy plumbago (C. plumbaginoides) by the upright shrubby form and slightly larger flowers. In autumn, the dark green foliage turns brilliant burgundy-red simultaneously with the last flush of flowers, creating an exceptional combination of blue flowers and red foliage. Hardy to zone 6, it dies back to the ground in cold winters and re-sprouts from the roots. In zone 9, it may remain evergreen in mild winters. Combines beautifully with ornamental grasses and late-season yellow wildflowers in fall gardens.
